story:
The town’s only charity shop, an old building near Marpleton’s priory ruins and watched by stone monks across the square, stayed dark one morning. Regulars peered in while a handful of helpers gathered in back. Patrick, the treasurer, had fetched spare keys and kept glancing at the locked cabinet of volunteer contacts. Everyone was anxious about one absence: Rhea, the manager who never missed a day. Ivy, a founder, swore Rhea’s New Zealand trip wasn’t due yet; the shop diary proved it. Calls to her landline and mobile went unanswered. Sylvana, a refugee who had grown close to Rhea, had knocked at her door with no reply, worry tightening into fear.

Patrick tried to be upbeat but suggested they might have to alert the Area Manager and perhaps ask Judd Digdeep, a recent volunteer, to help if the shop reopened. Sylvana said nothing, remembering her unease. Three months earlier Judd had arrived in a hoodie with a nervous cocker spaniel. Rhea, thorough but unsettled, had privately doubted his references. He proved strong with sacks and tidying, yet Sylvana had seen him with dubious company by the back door. Rhea had told her she’d keep an eye on it.

When days passed and Rhea still hadn’t appeared, Patrick took Sylvana to Rhea’s house with the help of a neighbor. Inside the spotless rooms, Sylvana found Rhea’s passport in a drawer and a set of shop keys—plus another ring tagged Cellar Keys. She also opened the birthday present Rhea had pressed on her days earlier: a pay-as-you-go phone already charged. Patrick, troubled by the passport, spoke of going to the police in the morning. Sylvana kept the cellar keys.

That night, drawing on habits from a stint at a detective agency in her home country, Sylvana slipped into the shop cellar wearing gloves. Damp air and a sour sweetness met her. In the dimness lay Judd, slumped with a bloodied head beside a shattered vase. Packets and tubs on shelves told a second story: the cellar had been turned into a drug stash. She touched nothing, locked up, and fled.

Halfway across the park her bag vibrated. Rhea’s voice, clear on the gifted phone, asked if Sylvana had found the keys—and Judd. They arranged to meet by the sea. When Rhea climbed up from the pier, Sylvana folded into her arms. Over hot fish and chips, Rhea told the truth. Judd was running a drug racket. He’d caught her in the cellar while she was searching for something she believed was hidden there—an old secret her historian father had hinted at. He threatened to torture his dog and harm her unless she let him use the space. Rhea, who had kept a second set of cellar keys at home, felt fear like she had once before. Days later, when she tried to stop him, he reached for a knife. She grabbed a dusty vase and struck, a split-second act of self-defence. Panicked, already reeling from a diagnosis of terminal illness she had learned of the day before his threat, she left Marpleton, giving Sylvana the phone so they could stay in touch.

Sylvana urged her to go to the police; Rhea couldn’t face them yet. She asked Sylvana to report the body without mentioning her, and pressed two envelopes into her hands: a copy of her will—“My final wish is to make your life better”—and her father’s plan of the cellar, marking a sealed entrance and a tunnel toward the priory. “What the monks left behind was more than history,” he’d said. Rhea asked Sylvana to stay the night, then return before questions mounted. The wind rose outside, and the tide carried its secrets away, while Sylvana held the map and the promise of a hidden passage, torn between duty, love, and the future Rhea still wanted her to claim.
